title: Flips and Rotations
concept: Flipping an image horizontally mirrors it left-to-right, while rotating turns it by a random angle. These are simple but powerful geometric changes, but you must be careful: some flips and rotations can change the actual meaning of an image.

exercises.prompt: Recap: How does data augmentation help prevent overfitting during training?

options.text: By showing the model slightly different variations of each image so it can't memorize them
By freezing the weights of the convolutional layers
By automatically deleting duplicate images from the dataset

exercises.explain: Yes! Varied images keep the model from memorizing exact training files.

cards.title: The Left-Facing Horse
cards.body: If every horse photo in your training set has the horse facing to the left, your model might struggle to recognize a horse facing right at deployment. A simple horizontal flip creates a right-facing horse instantly, correcting this bias.

cards.title: Horizontal Flips & Rotations
cards.body: Horizontal flipping mirrors an image left-to-right. Rotations turn the image by a random angle (e.g. ±15 degrees) around its center. These teach the model that object identities are independent of direction or tilt.

cards.title: Parking on a hill
cards.body: A car is still a car whether it's parked flat on a street or parked on a steep hill. Rotating an image slightly is like parking on a hill — it tilts the camera angle, ensuring the model doesn't expect every object to align perfectly with the horizon.

cards.title: Cats vs. Numbers
cards.body: Flipping a cat horizontally creates another valid cat. But flipping a handwritten number '3' horizontally turns it into a backwards symbol that is no longer a valid number. For digits, horizontal flipping is a bad idea!

cards.title: Don't flip vertically unless appropriate
cards.body: Vertical flipping (upside down) is rarely a good default. Flipping a house or a car upside down makes them look highly unrealistic, which can confuse a model training for real-world tasks. Only use vertical flips if the camera could naturally view the object upside down (like cells in a microscope).

left.text:
Natural scenes (forests, oceans)
Animals (dogs, cats, birds)
Faces, cars, everyday objects

right.text:
Text and documents (mirrors writing)
Handwritten digits (3, 5, etc.)
Road signs (like One Way indicators)

exercises.prompt: For which of these datasets would horizontal flipping be an UNSAFE augmentation technique?

options.text: A dataset of handwritten digits (0-9) to read ZIP codes
A dataset of natural forest images to detect fires
A dataset of pedestrian photos for self-driving cars
A dataset of product photos on a retail website

exercises.explain: Correct! Flipping digits like 3, 5, or 6 changes or destroys their meaning, creating invalid symbols.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Rotating an image by 10 degrees changes its pixel values without moving pixel positions.
exercises.explain: False. Rotation moves pixels to new positions in the frame, making it a geometric augmentation.

exercises.sentence: Horizontal flipping mirrors an image left-to-right, while ___ flipping turns it upside-down.
exercises.explain: Vertical flipping turns the image upside-down (rarely used unless objects can naturally appear upside-down).

exercises.prompt: Which of these is a valid reason to apply small random rotations during CNN training?

options.text: To teach the model to recognize objects even if the camera is slightly tilted
To permanently double the height and width of all training images
To completely erase the background of every image automatically

exercises.explain: Correct! Rotation helps the model handle tilted perspectives and camera angles.

exercises.prompt: A developer working on a self-driving car model decides to apply vertical flipping (upside down) to train a traffic light detector. What is the risk?

options.text: Traffic lights are always oriented vertically with red on top; flipping them upside down creates unrealistic examples that could confuse the model when detecting real upright traffic lights
Vertical flipping makes the training run twice as slow without changing the images at all
There is no risk because traffic lights frequently appear upside down in real traffic scenarios
Vertical flipping is unsafe because it changes the color of the traffic lights from red to green

exercises.explain: Exactly! Upside-down traffic lights don't happen in normal driving, so training on them adds useless noise. Next, let's explore cropping and random resizing!
